text:
在 AI 光通信乐观预期的推动下，Sivers Photonics 最近涨得很猛。但它大约 75% 的收入仍然来自无线毫米波业务，光子业务只是其中较小的一块。市场正在给它定价相当可观的光通信上行空间。真正的交易结构是这样的。

从收入结构来看：无线业务，也就是用于 5G 和 SATCOM 的毫米波波束成形器/组件，仍然占大头。光子业务才是这只股票正在折现的成长故事。

这个结构很重要，因为任何估值重估能否成立，取决于光学业务能不能真正放量，同时无线业务的基本盘不能掉下去。

目前光子业务主要由用于硅光收发器的 CW DFB （连续波分布反馈式激光器）激光器驱动。

Sivers 提供的是 50–75mW 器件，对 400G 和 800G 来说还不错，但明显低于 Lumentum 等公司为前沿应用或 CPO 应用提供的 300mW+ 功率水平。

这就是我们在 $AAOI 这类标的上见过的典型二线溢出逻辑：它不是技术领导者，但当一级供应商跟不上爆炸式增长的 AI 光模块需求时，“够用”的产能也会被拉进供应链。

这里真正具体的资产是 Glasgow 的 100mm InP 晶圆厂。按照利用率和产品组合不同，理论上的年度 CW 激光器收入潜力大约在 5000 万到 1.5 亿美元之间。

但问题在于：这是一座老旧的小型晶圆厂。我们没有看到激进扩产，也没有看到重大设备订单，能证明公司正在为更大规模的机会做准备。这限制了它实际承接溢出需求的速度。

更高 beta 的第二阶段，是它的多波长 DFB 激光阵列技术。

这项技术是为 CPO 和下一代光 I/O 所需的高密度、多波长需求专门设计的。公司已经展示过其与 Ayar Labs 配合运行的成果。

但它目前仍处于收入前阶段。Nvidia 和 Broadcom 正在开发自己的 CPO 方案，而更广泛的 CPO 采用时间表仍然不确定。所以这现在还只是纯粹的可选项，而不是基本盘收入。

结论：$SIVE 是一个押注两件事同时发生的 call option——当前 CW 激光器溢出周期继续延长，同时多波长阵列在 CPO 世界里获得真正 traction，并且无线业务不能下滑。

在最近大涨之后，这个门槛已经很高。产能执行和客户导入方面的风险是真实存在的。

这是典型的非对称交易结构：如果仓位控制得当，会很有意思；但如果你把光通信叙事当成已经完全去风险，那就很危险。

text:
The AI buildout has layers well beyond the GPU.

Photonics is the connective tissue - and the companies building it are scaling just as fast as the compute layer.

Follow me @JasonL_Capital for more deep dives on the infrastructure behind AI.

9. $AEHR - Aehr Test Systems

Every chip in this thread has to be tested before deployment. Aehr builds the burn-in and reliability test systems for AI processors, silicon photonics, and power semiconductors. Stock up over 200% in 2026. Expecting follow-on production orders from its lead hyperscale customer for custom AI processor testing.

2. $CRDO - Credo Technology

Builds the retimers, optical DSPs, and active electrical cables that maintain signal integrity at rack level inside AI clusters. Revenue of $407M last quarter, up 272% year over year. Acquired DustPhotonics to move into silicon photonics. Targeting over $500M in optical revenue in FY2027.

Goldman Sachs just projected the AI optical networking market hitting $154B by 2028 - nearly 10x from today.

As GPU clusters scale into multi-rack systems like NVIDIA's NVL576, the bottleneck shifts from compute to the fabric connecting it all.

10 companies across the photonics supply chain:

1. $MRVL - Marvell Technology

Designs the optical DSPs and custom interconnect silicon that encode and decode data moving between GPUs at 800G and 1.6T speeds. Data center revenue hit $1.5B last quarter - 74% of total revenue - up 38% year over year. Record quarterly revenue of $2.075B.

text:
the photonics layer is where the ai buildout gets really interesting.

goldman has the ai optical networking market going almost 10x to $150B+ by 2028 as training clusters move from single-rack boxes to multi-rack systems like $NVDA rubin ultra nvl576.

that matters because the bottleneck is shifting. less about just packing in more gpus, more about the fabric that makes hundreds of gpus act like one machine.

that shift drives a lot more dollar content into nvlink, co-packaged optics, and optical interconnects.

the way i’d map it:
• #AEHR for burn-in and reliability test gear used in photonics and ai hardware
• #AAOI for datacenter optical transceivers, with the in-house laser piece being the key differentiator
• #LITE for high-speed lasers and optical components inside datacenter transceivers
• #VIAV for optical test and measurement tools that validate these higher-speed networks
• #COHR for lasers, photonic components, and materials across optical systems
• #MRVL for optical dsps, custom interconnect silicon, and networking chips
• #CRDO for retimers, dsps, and active electrical cables that help preserve signal integrity at the rack level

most people still look at ai infra through the compute lens only. i think the fabric layer is where the next content expansion shows up.

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
『シリコンフォトニクスってよく聞くけど、実際に何ができるの？』

Xscape Photonicsというスタートアップが面白いんだ。Broadcomで18年以上、光集積回路の開発をリードしてた人が立ち上げた会社なんだよ。

シリコンフォトニクスっていうのは、シリコンチップの上に「光」の配線を作る技術のこと。今までは電気で信号を送ってたけど、光なら超高速で大量のデータが運べるんだ。

これが面白いのは、CPO（光トランシーバー）みたいな外付けの光チップじゃなくて、プロセッサーそのものに光を組み込もうってアプローチ。電気配線の限界をスキップできるわけだね。

2026年のいま、各社がこぞって光を研究してるのはこういう背景があるからなんだ。Broadcom出身の経験者が独立起業できるってことは、市場がそれだけ大きくなった証拠。

→ NISAで半導体を長期保有するなら、こういった部材・基盤技術の進化の方が株価より先に動くんだ。

text:
If you didn't know...I'm very bullish on $IQE

$MTSI  £45M investment materially cleans up the balance sheet + de-risks operations.

It allows them to repay debt + fund core capex. And allows them to drive capacity towards more photonics stuff.

As a standalone catalyst, that's huge for a company at a tiny MC of just £448M.

So they've got Macom as a customer now via LTAs, and $LITE as their flagship customer too.

And you've got huge institutions like Lombard Odier, Artisan, and $IBKR as significant shareholders.

I expected the $MTSI dilution to force the share price down to the low 30p level - which we hit last week.

So looks like dilution may have already happened if the new shares hit the register sooner than I thought initially.

Imo, the significantly de-risked business post-fundraise deserves a material re-rate to more premium valuations.

text:
FYI,
It's not the cost of AI to run it.

We run it down the line on self-generating photonics.
👆🏼
I want you to listen to what I'm telling you.

READ THAT AGAIN.

Because if you don't you will not recognize the biggest sleight of hand in your lifetimes and the consequences are quantified.

This is a pivotal moment.

What is energy?
What are you?

What is 'cost effective' ?

YOU ARE POWER.

Knowing your future to change it
is not a prediction.

You are perpetually and eternally connected to your future which is the past understanding itself and its journey (🫵🏼🪞🪩), confusing it with true future (God/unknown).

The past holds the pattern...
Don't forego, lest you forget yourselves and lose the precious gift God created in you, as you, for time and space, with time and space, from time and space..

We are all connected.
Love one another past the gods who would define you through their need of identification.

True God is unknowable and undefinable... and in that, omnipresent and omnipotent and eternal.

Trust - you are more than energy.

Own your soul by being responsible for it.

God will not do for you what you can do for yourselves - you are meant to grow - not to become consumed or subsumed into matrices.

God will also not give you what you cannot handle.
Good and bad.

We keep ourselves by remembering each other.

Patterns.
Past and Future converge over one point.. Awareness = Now 

Wake up, John.
Mourning bells are ringing.

MssCorp (https://t.co/MMqfwsnwyH)  2026/5/27 earnings presentation leak :

-Self-developed MSS HG (Helmet Gecko) Silicon Photonics tester:

• Three HG units have been assembled and are now operational, providing silicon photonics R&D analysis and engineering validation services to multiple clients.
• The core optical loss detection technology has received patents in Taiwan, Japan, and the United States.
• The system has successfully completed certification by major US AI chip manufacturers.
• PD and QA production versions are scheduled for launch in 2026.
• High-volume commercial ramp-up is expected in 2027.
This development represents one of the company’s four key growth engines, advancing the transition from analysis services to a higher-margin business model encompassing equipment sales, IP licensing, and integrated measurement services in the expanding CPO/silicon photonics market.

POET's passive approach runs at standard semiconductor speeds, so you're talking seconds instead of minutes. That's huge for volume production. Roughly 2 orders of magnitude (100x) improvement per device in effective alignment/assembly time. ￼ Why ~2 orders of magnitude? • Traditional active alignment (most photonics competitors): Requires powering up the device + real-time feedback loops to optimize coupling. This is typically done serially (one module at a time) and takes tens of seconds to several minutes per device/module. ￼ • POET passive alignment: Mechanically self-aligning via pre-etched features on the wafer. Uses standard semiconductor pick-and-place tools — ~0.1–few seconds per component placement, done in a highly parallel wafer-level process (500+ engines per 8” wafer)."}}}, "rest_id": "2058429967827079439", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2547", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2059055899705332000", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "124", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Feels like the market is hunting for anything tied to the AI infrastructure and photonics buildout right now, especially smaller high beta names.

Interesting seeing capital rotate further down the stack too. $XMAX has started popping up more for a similar reason with the AI infrastructure narrative plus the indirect SpaceX and xAI exposure layered on top.

$LPTH deep dive:

LightPath is a real little defense/edge-optics story, not just another “AI photonics” ticker someone found under a couch cushion.

Q3 FY26 revenue was USD 19.1M, +109% YoY. Gross profit was USD 7.0M, +161%. Gross margin hit 36%, adjusted EBITDA was positive at USD 1.1M, and backlog reached roughly USD 110.6M, up 196% from June 2025.

The thesis: BlackDiamond germanium-free infrared optics, G5 imaging systems, and U.S. optical manufacturing could become useful in counter-drone, ISR, border, weapons, and autonomous sensing programs. This is physical AI / defense-sensor plumbing, not data-center CPO.

The data is good enough to care. TTM revenue is only about USD 62.8M, but backlog is already more than 1.7x TTM sales. Management is targeting more than USD 300M of revenue within five years. Consensus has FY26 revenue around USD 72.7M and FY27 around USD 101.3M, so estimate revisions are the actual battleground.

Top catalysts I care about:

1. Q4/FY27 guide proving backlog converts into revenue and margin.
2. A named defense program or prime/customer order above USD 20M.
3. BlackDiamond / second U.S. glass capacity turning from slide-deck promise into qualified throughput and orders.

Risks: the stock already ripped to USD 16.95 after a +18.7% day, above the USD 15.98 average sell-side target and basically at the USD 17 high target. GAAP losses and negative FCF still matter. Defense procurement can turn “pipeline” into “please wait 18 months.” And the social signal is still mostly single-source.

Entry/Verdict: CATALYST WATCH / no chase. Optional 0.25%-0.50% scout only if you want defense-optics optionality. Add-size only after backlog conversion, >35% gross margin, positive adjusted EBITDA, or a named USD 20M+ defense order.

I like the proof. I hate paying victory-lap prices before the next contract shows up. We'll see how it goes.

text:
Here’s my long-winded post on $SIVE for the weekend.

Next week is bloody important. ER hits, MSCI inclusion soon after. 

$SIVE Catalysts:

Sivers Semiconductors is a Swedish leader in photonics (high-power lasers for AI data centers/co-packaged optics) and wireless/mmWave tech (5G/6G, SATCOM, beamformers). 

The company is riding multiple tailwinds in AI infrastructure, defense, and transatlantic tech cooperation.

Key Recent & Upcoming Catalysts:

1. CHIPS Act Funding (US Strategic Validation)
•  Sivers has secured awards through the Northeast Microelectronics Coalition (NEMC) Hub under the US CHIPS and Science Act (Microelectronics Commons program).
•  Awards include ~$6M+ initial funding for 5G/6G beamformer ICs/array design (FR3 frequencies) and Electronic Warfare projects, with potential to reach ~$11.6M+ in year 1 and up to ~$30M over multiple years if extended. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Partners include major players like Ericsson, Raytheon, BAE Systems, MIT, and others. This de-risks development, funds US-aligned prototyping, and strengthens Sivers’ position in secure supply chains for defense and advanced comms.

2. White House / US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal
•  Recent signing of the US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal (highlighted in White House releases and statements by Secretary of State Rubio) deepens cooperation in AI, 5G/6G, biotech, quantum, defense tech, advanced manufacturing, and trusted infrastructure. 
•  This bilateral push aligns perfectly with Sivers’ portfolio in AI optics, lasers, photonics, and wireless tech. It signals stronger US-Sweden tech ties, potential for more collaboration/funding, and elevated strategic importance for Swedish innovators like Sivers. 

3. Nasdaq Dual Listing Evaluation
•  In April 2026, Sivers announced it’s evaluating a dual listing on Nasdaq New York (while keeping Swedish domicile). This aims to tap deeper US capital markets, broaden the investor base, and support growth in AI/photonics. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Preparations include audit uplifts to US standards (PCAOB). Positive for liquidity, visibility, and valuation re-rating as US investors gain easier access.

4. Other Strong Tailwinds:
•  AI Optics Boom: High-power lasers for co-packaged optics in next-gen AI data centers. Partnerships (e.g., O-Net, POET) targeting volume production in late 2026. Strong demand with structural shortages noted by peers. @i 
•  Pipeline growth: ~$453M (up 64% YoY), product revenue mix improving significantly. @hoarderresearch 
•  Q1 results expected May 29, 2026 – potential positive print catalyst.
•  Index inclusions and overall sector momentum in AI/semiconductors.
$SIVE has seen sharp moves recently on AI/photonics hype, but remains a smaller-cap story with real execution and policy support behind it. The combination of CHIPS funding, US-Sweden tech pact, Nasdaq ambitions, and AI optics exposure creates a compelling multi-catalyst setup.
Not financial advice – DYOR. Markets are volatile, especially in small-cap tech/semiconductors.

What are your thoughts on $SIVE? 

Holding or watching? 🚀

Next week is bloody important. ER hits, MSCI inclusion soon after. $SIVE Catalysts: Sivers Semiconductors is a Swedish leader in photonics (high-power lasers for AI data centers/co-packaged optics) and wireless/mmWave tech (5G/6G, SATCOM, beamformers). The company is riding multiple tailwinds in AI infrastructure, defense, and transatlantic tech cooperation. Key Recent & Upcoming Catalysts: 1. CHIPS Act Funding (US Strategic Validation) • Sivers has secured awards through the Northeast Microelectronics Coalition (NEMC) Hub under the US CHIPS and Science Act (Microelectronics Commons program). • Awards include ~$6M+ initial funding for 5G/6G beamformer ICs/array design (FR3 frequencies) and Electronic Warfare projects, with potential to reach ~$11.6M+ in year 1 and up to ~$30M over multiple years if extended. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 • Partners include major players like Ericsson, Raytheon, BAE Systems, MIT, and others. This de-risks development, funds US-aligned prototyping, and strengthens Sivers’ position in secure supply chains for defense and advanced comms. 2. White House / US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal • Recent signing of the US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal (highlighted in White House releases and statements by Secretary of State Rubio) deepens cooperation in AI, 5G/6G, biotech, quantum, defense tech, advanced manufacturing, and trusted infrastructure. • This bilateral push aligns perfectly with Sivers’ portfolio in AI optics, lasers, photonics, and wireless tech. It signals stronger US-Sweden tech ties, potential for more collaboration/funding, and elevated strategic importance for Swedish innovators like Sivers. 3. Nasdaq Dual Listing Evaluation • In April 2026, Sivers announced it’s evaluating a dual listing on Nasdaq New York (while keeping Swedish domicile). This aims to tap deeper US capital markets, broaden the investor base, and support growth in AI/photonics. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 • Preparations include audit uplifts to US standards (PCAOB). Positive for liquidity, visibility, and valuation re-rating as US investors gain easier access. 4. Other Strong Tailwinds: • AI Optics Boom: High-power lasers for co-packaged optics in next-gen AI data centers. Partnerships (e.g., O-Net, POET) targeting volume production in late 2026. Strong demand with structural shortages noted by peers. @i • Pipeline growth: ~$453M (up 64% YoY), product revenue mix improving significantly. @hoarderresearch • Q1 results expected May 29, 2026 – potential positive print catalyst. • Index inclusions and overall sector momentum in AI/semiconductors. $SIVE has seen sharp moves recently on AI/photonics hype, but remains a smaller-cap story with real execution and policy support behind it. The combination of CHIPS funding, US-Sweden tech pact, Nasdaq ambitions, and AI optics exposure creates a compelling multi-catalyst setup. Not financial advice – DYOR. Markets are volatile, especially in small-cap tech/semiconductors. What are your thoughts on $SIVE? Historically, that is not early-cycle behavior. This is late-cycle behavior.

$PENG - PENGUIN SOLUTIONS IS AN AI-FACTORY PLATFORM AT A $2B MARKET CAP WITH BILLIONS IN REVENUE, $100M FCF, AND A FORWARD P/E UNDER 17×

Penguin Solutions, formerly SMART Global Holdings, integrates AI factory platforms across photonics, CPU, memory, and AI infrastructure. The stock closed Tuesday near $55 after running +80% in the last month — a tape that has noticed but a multiple that has not yet rerated to peers. $100M in free cash flow and a sub-17× forward P/E in a category where AI-factory comparable names trade at 30-50× revenue is the cleanest mispricing left in the $2B-cap AI-infra integrator bucket.

Mechanism: this is the picks-and-shovels expression of the same buildout driving NBIS, CRWV, and the neocloud cohort, but with positive free cash flow today instead of negative operating margins funded by debt. As hyperscalers and Tier-2 AI providers push integrators for prepackaged photonics + memory + power stacks, a profitable platform vendor at $2B mcap is a takeout candidate before it ever needs another capital raise.

text:
Space, Satcom, and HPC diverging positively in flat tape. Good spot to look for pullbacks in the coming days.

Photonics pulled back to support levels and held. Will see if they continue to hold, I am a long a basket here and should focus capital elsewhere or look for adds.

Chip testing and Semi infra had some recent weakness and is recovering, offering a couple days of sideways digestion that could offer some nice entry points. Some areas of memory also digesting recent gains (MRAM) that be good r/r entries.

text:
I see a lot of people saying Sivers is not a bottleneck so I wanted to write a quick thread on what actually makes something a bottleneck in photonics — and why $SIVE fits, even if many people say it doesn’t.

A lot of people get confused and think bottleneck is just “being the only supplier.” instead, It’s when a specific component is difficult and slow to replace at scale, because of technical complexity, long qualification times, or limited alternatives that meet the required performance.

Many people look at SIVE and say “it’s not a bottleneck” because:
- It doesn’t own its own high-volume fab
- There are other laser companies (Lumentum $LITE , Coherent $COHR , etc.)
- Hyperscalers usually multi-source

However, that misses the point:
SIVE’s position is narrow but very meaningful because very few suppliers can deliver the specific combination of power, wavelength stability, and thermal performance that several CPO architectures require.

Once these lasers are qualified into a system like $SIVE is with  (Ayar, Jabil ecosystem, $POET, etc.), switching is slow and risky. Qualification cycles in this layer are long. That friction is what creates a bottleneck — even without a full monopoly.

Here an image showing what makes $SIVE's lasers different from the others and hard to replace

text:
2. GPU와 스위치 성능은 계속 올라가는데

전기 신호 기반 연결은 거리와 발열 부담이 커지고 있어서 

광 I/O로 더 가까이 붙는 방식이 필요해진 것임. 

인텔은 4Tbps급 광 I/O 칩렛(chiplet, 기능별로 쪼갠 소형 반도체 블록)을 공개했고

엔비디아도 실리콘 포토닉스(silicon photonics, 반도체 위에서 빛으로 데이터를 전송하는 기술) 기반 네트워크를 차세대 AI 인프라 축으로 제시했음.

3. 가장 위쪽은 스위치 칩과 메인 프로세서 영역임. 

이 구간은 데이터 흐름을 직접 제어하는 두뇌 역할을 맡고

관련 기업은 $NVDA (엔비디아), $AVGO (브로드컴), $AMD (AMD), $MRVL (마벨), $GOOGL (알파벳)임. 

브로드컴은 51.2Tbps급 Bailly CPO 스위치를 고객사에 공급했다고 밝혔고

엔비디아는 Spectrum-X와 Quantum-X 포토닉스 스위치를 통해 대규모 GPU 연결 효율 개선을 강조하고 있음.

4. 그다음은 광엔진(optical engine, 전기 신호를 빛으로 바꾸고 다시 전기로 되돌리는 핵심 광모듈)임. 

CPO에서 사실상 성능과 전력 효율을 좌우하는 본체에 가까우며,

포토닉 집적회로(PIC, 광신호를 처리하는 집적칩), 변조기(modulator, 빛에 데이터를 실어 보내는 장치), 수광부가 함께 묶여 동작함. 

이 구간은 $TSM (TSMC), $INTC (인텔), $005930.KS (삼성전자), $NVDA (엔비디아), $AVGO (브로드컴)이 연결되며

TSMC는 COUPE 플랫폼을 통해 실리콘 포토닉스 패키징 양산 로드맵을 제시한 상태임.

(이어서 계속 🔗)

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
『シリコンフォトニクスって、結局AIチップと何が関係あるの？』 そんなあなたへ。Xscape Photonicsという新しい企業が、BroadcomにいたVivek氏を迎えたんだ。彼は18年間、統合フォトニクス（光を使ってチップ同士をつなぐ技術）の最前線にいた人物だよ。

なぜこれが今重要か。2026年のいま、NVIDIAのBlackwell Ultraの次の課題は『データセンター内の配線』なんだ。銅配線じゃスピードが追いつかない。光配線に切り替える必要がある。その時に必要なのが、シリコンフォトニクスチップ。

Broadcomはこの分野で先行していたけど、Xscape Photonicsは『チップレベルの統合』でアプローチを変えようとしている。つまり、次の技術覇権争いがここで始まってるんだ。→ 半導体投資家にとっては、光デバイス企業（LumentumやCoherent）の動きだけじゃなく、『光チップを誰が作るか』の競争構図が読めると、2026年後半の戦略が見えてくるよ。

text:
Power consumption per person has been nearly static since 2007 in the US and per person we use far more devices. Smaller and more efficient. 

when I say photonics are more efficient I am talking about both power consumption and processing power. A photonic chip does more work with a fraction of the power and produces very little heat, so reducing power needs even further. 

A Nvidia Blackwell GPU uses about 700 watts per chip, a photonic chip uses 50–100 watts to do the same amount of work. It is still in developmental stages, but they have working photonic GPUs and hybrid GPUs that are dramatically more efficient per watt. 

Now photonics are not in mass production yet, but they have working GPUs right now and are working on building data center scale sites..

Now take the Colossus center planned to use 1 million Nvidia Blackwell GPUs. that is 700 megawatts for just the chips, add in cooling and everything else and you are talking about around 1 gigawatt. 

The same center using Photonics can process more data, faster and only use around 140-150 megawatts total.. That is not some day tech, they have working processors and networks setup right now...

text:
I think you misunderstand the point. Photonics are not about storing information, but processing. Quartz and other medium have an information density that is insane. 

Take the US as an example, power use peaked in 2007, and Per capita and per household electricity (all energy consumption really) has flattened and even declined a little.

We can do way more effective work with less power today than we could 20 years ago. Your phone uses a tiny fraction of the power a supercomputer in the 80's or 90's did and can process more information. This is the tend of technology, not bigger and more power hungry, but smaller and more efficient. Any other civilization that was following the same basic technology direction would be the same. 

Per watt hour we produce 1.5-2 x more today than in 1990... Smaller more efficient. 

Power stations are getting smaller and producing more, Nuclear power is doing the same. We have micro reactors that can produce 1-20 MWe in something the size of a cargo container... 

Everything is getting smaller and more efficient. 

As for best source? Fusion or a fusion / fission breeder reactor. There are other theoretical methods, zero point etc, but what is practical? Fusion. You are creating a micro star, and once we have that it will just get smaller and more efficient producing more power. However behind all of that will be technology that gets smaller and more efficient. 

With the current trend within 200 years per watt hour we will produce 10 x or more per watt hour. 

As an example a data center like Colossus using photonics will have vastly more computing power and use a fraction of the electricity it does today with photonics. Some chips are 100 x more efficient, meaning they use almost no power in comparison to a conventional silicon chip. and they are not a few centuries out, they are making working models right now... 

A cell phone with photonics would last over a week, massively increase capabilities all using the exact same battery it uses right now. 

Dyson anything - Also pointless. Fusion removes any point of creating them and the cost in resources, processing, deploying and maintaining even a swarm is so insane. By the time we got net positive power it would be centuries down the road, and just to produce one you would have to have a better source of power just to be able to mine on a planetary scale, transport, refine, deploy, repair and convert that power. 

Even Dyson called it a thought exercise, but the logistics of it are a net negative. 

I like how you are thinking, but the logistics just isn't there, and by the time we could do that, we will have no need of doing it.

Photonics are not about storing information, but processing. Quartz and other medium have an information density that is insane. Take the US as an example, power use peaked in 2007, and Per capita and per household electricity (all energy consumption really) has flattened and even declined a little. We can do way more effective work with less power today than we could 20 years ago. Your phone uses a tiny fraction of the power a supercomputer in the 80's or 90's did and can process more information. This is the tend of technology, not bigger and more power hungry, but smaller and more efficient. Any other civilization that was following the same basic technology direction would be the same. Per watt hour we produce 1.5-2 x more today than in 1990... Smaller more efficient. Power stations are getting smaller and producing more, Nuclear power is doing the same. We have micro reactors that can produce 1-20 MWe in something the size of a cargo container... Everything is getting smaller and more efficient. As for best source? Fusion or a fusion / fission breeder reactor. There are other theoretical methods, zero point etc, but what is practical? Fusion. You are creating a micro star, and once we have that it will just get smaller and more efficient producing more power. However behind all of that will be technology that gets smaller and more efficient. With the current trend within 200 years per watt hour we will produce 10 x or more per watt hour. As an example a data center like Colossus using photonics will have vastly more computing power and use a fraction of the electricity it does today with photonics. Some chips are 100 x more efficient, meaning they use almost no power in comparison to a conventional silicon chip. and they are not a few centuries out, they are making working models right now... A cell phone with photonics would last over a week, massively increase capabilities all using the exact same battery it uses right now. Dyson anything - Also pointless. Fusion removes any point of creating them and the cost in resources, processing, deploying and maintaining even a swarm is so insane. By the time we got net positive power it would be centuries down the road, and just to produce one you would have to have a better source of power just to be able to mine on a planetary scale, transport, refine, deploy, repair and convert that power. Even Dyson called it a thought exercise, but the logistics of it are a net negative. I like how you are thinking, but the logistics just isn't there, and by the time we could do that, we will have no need of doing it."}}}, "rest_id": "2058648951805616615", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "37", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Huge move, but this is exactly where discipline matters.

The story is strong: AI data centres, photonics, US listing potential, and a growing pipeline.

But after a 3,000% run, we shift towards "how much of the future is already priced in?"

Pipeline ≠ revenue yet. I’d be watching execution, margins, cash burn, and the insider investigation closely.

text:
A team at Westlake University in China just published a handheld cancer detector in Nature Photonics. Single drop of blood, 94.9% accuracy for early-stage lung cancer, 10,000× more sensitive than the standard lab test.

Chip cost: $5.Worth paying attention to, but also worth reading carefully. That 94.9% comes from 171 patient samples. The device replaces refrigerator-sized lab equipment with an LED, a photodetector, and a 3D metamaterial chip. Genuinely impressive physics.

However, the gap nobody's talking about: every year produces 3-4 "revolutionary cancer detection" papers. Almost none survive the jump from Nature paper to clinical validation at scale.

Caveat here: 171 patients is a starting point, not proof. But the engineering approach is what's worth watching.

text:
The AI bottlenecks that are forming as we speak

$MRVL supplies critical connectivity silicon. $COHR is critical for its industrial-scale photonics manufacturing. Big backing by $NVDA. $LITE is critical for its continuous wave laser components and early high-speed lane adoption. Also backed by $NVDA.

For bandwidth and capacity limits, $ALAB has a dominant position for scaling up AI clusters.

For cooling, $VRT has been the dominate leader for the data center cooling market.

For grid exhaustion, $CEG is the leader in dedicated nuclear generation and is the largest pure-play carbon-free nuclear operator. $VST has decade-long agreements with hyperscalers too for nuclear power. $OKLO is also a strong contender for dedicated nuclear generation.

$AVGO is the critical enabler of the ASIC revolution. They are engineering custom silicon to minimize power and interconnect penalties. 

$CBRS is proposing a way to bypass the interconnect wall by utilizing wafer-scale alternatives. This bypasses the need for co-packaged optics by manufacturing AI chips as a single, massive silicon wafers, which keeps the pathways fully on chip.

text:
THE PHOTONICS LAYER OF THE AI BUILDOUT

Goldman Sachs says the AI optical networking market growing nearly 10x to $150B+ by 2028 as clusters scale into multi-rack systems like $NVDA Rubin Ultra NVL576.

The AI bottleneck is shifting to the fabric that lets hundreds of GPUs behave like one system driving a massive dollar content expansion across NVLink, co-packaged optics & optical interconnects: 

• $AEHR burn-in & reliability test systems for photonics and AI hardware
• $AAOI data center optical transceivers with in-house laser manufacturing
• $LITE high-speed lasers & optical components for data center transceivers
• $VIAV optical testing & measurement tools used to validate high-speed networks
• $COHR lasers, photonic components & materials used across optical networking systems
• $MRVL optical DSPs, custom interconnect silicon & networking chips for AI infrastructure
• $CRDO retimers, DSPs & active electrical cables for rack-level signal integrity in AI clusters https://t.co/70aHTSwMZa

Japan has the parts of the stack that sound too boring (until the shipment is late) 4062.T Ibiden: the substrate floor under high-end AI packages. 2802.T Ajinomoto: the soup and MSG company behind Ajinomoto Build-up Film, the insulating film inside those substrates. 4063.T Shin-Etsu: wafers and materials before the chip is even a chip. 8035.T Tokyo Electron: process tools that make advanced manufacturing repeatable. 5332.T TOTO: ceramics. 3110.T Nittobo: glass fiber. 4004.T Resonac: specialty materials and packaging-adjacent chemistry. JSR matters too, but it is not a clean public ticker anymore after the 2024 delisting. That almost makes the point better imo. Some of the most important AI infrastructure is already disappearing from the easy screen. pattern is simple: AI looks American at the model layer. looks Taiwanese at the foundry layer. looks Korean at the memory layer. Then you go one layer lower and the map starts turning Japanese. Films. Fibers. Ceramics. Resists. Substrates. Tools. text:
$SIVE

The White House just put US-Sweden cooperation around AI, 5G/6G, cloud, electronics, defense tech, advanced manufacturing and trusted infrastructure on paper.

That matters.

Because this is no longer just a “small Swedish photonics story.”

This is starting to become a strategic infrastructure story.

Sivers is exposed to the exact layers the US and Sweden now want to deepen:

AI data center optics
laser sources
advanced connectivity
defense-grade photonics
trusted tech supply chains
US capital market optionality

The market may still see $SIVE as a tiny Nordic semiconductor name.

I think that is the opportunity.

Photonic chokepoints are moving from niche to national strategy.

text:
$SIVE Sivers Semiconductors: Strategic Validation Across AI Photonics, RF and Defense Infrastructure

https://t.co/EnmkiWUSEN

This $6.6M Year 2 EW STAR award is a very important signal, in my view.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem.

Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker.

What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening.

This is not only AI/datacenter photonics.

It is AI photonics plus InP lasers, SATCOM/LEO connectivity, advanced RF, beamforming, defense communications and potential U.S. institutional access.

That combination can become extremely powerful if execution continues.

Many semiconductor companies have one promising vertical. Sivers appears to be building relevance across several strategic markets at the same time — each tied to bandwidth, latency, power efficiency, secure connectivity and national security.

That is why I believe the market may still be far from fully pricing in the strategic value of this company.

The award is not just funding.

It is validation that Sivers is being pulled deeper into critical infrastructure layers where specialized semiconductor technology matters.

text:
$IREN is my third largest position.  But I may not have taken power seriously enough.

All roads lead to $SATS / $SPCX - another bonus for owning pre IPO.  Photonics could ultimately save 40% of the power but that will be on chips that consume much more than Rubin.

But the real shocker in this Morgan Stanley slide is actually power, because the industry is now talking about moving from roughly 120kW per rack today toward potentially 600kW per rack by the Vera Rubin Ultra generation in 2027, which is an almost unimaginable escalation in power density within an incredibly short period of time.

To put this into perspective, many traditional enterprise datacenters historically operated at only a few kilowatts per rack, while even modern hyperscale campuses today often consume only tens of megawatts in total facility power draw. But once you begin deploying hundreds or thousands of 600kW AI racks simultaneously, the math becomes almost absurd because a large-scale Vera Rubin Ultra cluster could eventually consume gigawatts of electricity, effectively rivaling the energy demand of a mid-sized city.

And this is where the market still massively underestimates the second-order implications of the AI boom, because the bottleneck is no longer simply semiconductors, GPUs, or memory supply. The bottleneck increasingly becomes electricity itself.

The US power grid can barely keep up with current AI infrastructure demand already, while transmission congestion, transformer shortages, substation constraints, cooling limitations, permitting bottlenecks, and aging grid infrastructure are becoming increasingly visible across major datacenter hubs. Importantly, grid infrastructure cannot scale at semiconductor speed. You can accelerate chip production with enough capital expenditure and engineering talent, but building transmission lines, substations, generation capacity, cooling systems, and interconnection approvals often requires many years due to environmental reviews, local opposition, labor shortages, and physical construction constraints.

This is precisely why we continue believing the AI buildout is not a two-to-three-year investment cycle, but instead a decade-long industrial transformation that increasingly resembles the buildout of railroads, electricity networks, and telecom infrastructure during previous industrial revolutions.

And this is also why energy infrastructure is quietly becoming one of the most important and underappreciated AI trades globally.

The winners are no longer just GPU companies. The winners increasingly include utilities like Constellation Energy and Vistra, nuclear-related plays like Oklo and NuScale Power, gas infrastructure companies like Kinder Morgan and Williams Companies, grid and electrical equipment suppliers like GE Vernova, Eaton, Schneider Electric, and Vertiv, as well as transformer, cooling, and datacenter infrastructure providers that now sit directly inside the physical backbone required to support next-generation compute.

Hyperscalers themselves are starting to understand this reality. Companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are no longer simply software companies buying servers. They are increasingly becoming quasi-energy infrastructure companies because securing long-duration power availability is becoming strategically inseparable from securing compute capacity itself.

That is why nuclear power is quietly returning to the center of the conversation. Hyperscalers may eventually fund or directly partner on nuclear generation projects out of pure necessity because renewable intermittency alone cannot reliably support ultra-high-density AI clusters operating continuously at scale.

In many ways, AI is beginning to collide with physical reality. You cannot run trillion-dollar next-generation compute infrastructure on transmission systems and grid architectures that were largely built decades ago for a completely different industrial era.

The semiconductor story may have started the AI race, but energy infrastructure may ultimately determine who wins it."}}}, "rest_id": "2057723120103170197", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "224147", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057899801945973187", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1089", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
INTERVIEW ON CPO: Radha Nagarajan, the Senior Vice President and Chief Technology Officer of Marvell’s Optical Engineering Group. 

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules?

I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market.

Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons?

If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset.

Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well.

Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration?

I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. 

Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need?

The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications.

Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs?

In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions.

However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out.

We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away.

That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation.

Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that?

That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here.

Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO?

We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well.

There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it.

So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems.

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules? I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market. Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons? If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset. Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well. Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration? I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. text:
This bull market won't last forever.

You need to take advantage of it while it's here.

Trillion-dollar industries are unfolding before our eyes:
AI, semis, photonics, space, data centers, energy, robotics, nuclear, drones, quantum.

The scale of change is massive, and the potential is enormous.

This bull markets is creating life-changing opportunities.

So, focus on the leading sectors and stocks. Be aggressive in the right areas, but don't get reckless. Always manage risk.

Moments like these are rare.

This is where fortunes are made.

Don't waste it.

text:
$SIVE and $AAPL.

Speculative post — no announced partnership exists. But the pattern is worth looking at.

People are noticing the same thing: Apple is quietly photonics-pilling its entire product stack.

→ Apple Watch — Long-rumored non-invasive glucose monitoring uses absorption spectroscopy. That requires tunable laser sources, not just standard VCSELs.

→ AirPods with cameras — The latest leak. Camera-enabled, always-on AI vision = depth sensors, time-of-flight components, low-power optical transmission.

→ iPhone LiDAR + Face ID — Already heavy on photonics. Next-gen sensing pushes toward higher resolution and longer range.

→ Vision Pro + future AR glasses
 — Eye tracking, depth perception, waveguide displays. All photonics-intensive.

→ Apple Intelligence servers — Apple is building its own AI infrastructure for Private Cloud Compute. Those datacenters need CPO, optical interconnects, External Light Sources — the exact stack $SIVE is built around.

Five surfaces. Five different photonics requirements. And Apple has historically diversified suppliers aggressively — they don't want permanent single-source dependency on Lumentum and Coherent.

Honest read of where $SIVE actually fits:
The simple VCSEL sensor work in current AirPods / Apple Watch is dominated by Lumentum and Coherent. That's not $SIVE's architecture.

But:
→ For LiDAR — $SIVE has a confirmed laser source program ramping Q4 2026.
→ For optical interconnects in Apple-built AI infrastructure — $SIVE sits in the same supply chain as Jabil and Ayar Labs.
→ For next-gen wearable sensing (glucose, advanced biometrics) — tunable InP-based lasers become more relevant as resolution and sensitivity requirements scale up. That IS $SIVE's architecture.

No claims of an existing relationship. Just an observation:

Apple is building photonics into five product lines at once. $SIVE happens to sit on the supply side of three of them.

text: 3/ The mix is the story. Photonics +15% to £57.1m and now the majority of revenue. Wireless -40% to £40.1m. And it's accelerating: H2 photonics £30.5m vs H1 £26.6m, wireless £21.5m vs £18.6m. text:
A major and largely overlooked fundamental catalyst for $SIVE is its strategic alliance with $ENAFF and O-Net Technologies.

This tripartite cooperation directly solves the biggest commercial bottleneck in silicon photonics: the highly complex and expensive optical packaging process, which historically accounted for up to 80% of total system costs.

And I personally have plans to acquire more ownership + support https://t.co/Mb5eRuL2N7

text:
本人持有的 serenity系列的股票：

1. $sive： Wireless 做 5G 毫米波芯片（RFIC、波束成形），Photonics 做 III-V 族化合物半导体激光器件，用于光纤通信和 AI 数据中心光互连，位于cpo上游InP DFB 激光阵列的关键供应商。

2. $hmdpf ： 加拿大电力变压器制造商。

3. $sloif： 全球 SOI（绝缘体上硅）晶圆的领导者

4.  $lpk ： 核心技术是 LIDE（激光诱导深度蚀刻），可在玻璃上精密加工通孔而不开裂，被视为下一代 AI 芯片先进封装（玻璃基板）的关键制造设备提供商。

5. $alrib ：法国分子束外延（MBE）设备制造商，生产用于化合物半导体材料生长的 MBE 系统，是 III-V 族半导体（GaAs、InP、GaN 等）生产的上游设备供应商，客户涵盖科研机构和工业客户。

6. $iqe ：英国化合物半导体外延晶圆（epiwafer）的全球最大独立供应商

7. $towa ：日本半导体封装设备制造商。

8. foci ：台湾光纤无源器件及集成模块制造商，产品涵盖光纤连接器、耦合器、WDM、有源光缆（AOC）等

9. shunsin：台湾 SiP（系统级封装）模组制造商，是富士康旗下子公司，主营无线通信模组（RF 前端/WiFi）、光纤收发模组、MEMS 传感器等的封装测试，客户涵盖消费电子和云服务器领域。

小盘的就这些吧 都买的不多。

text:
I can’t really comment on the competition we are hyper focused on what we are building. (Sometimes I have to stay politically correct… otherwise I could simply say: Joker. 🙈)

That said, photonics is an incredibly exciting evolution for the industry. I genuinely can’t wait to see it deployed at real hyperscale.

It will absolutely mitigate part of the thermal and power efficiency challenges tied to data movement and interconnects. But the reality remains the same: gigawatts are still gigawatts.

Physics does not negotiate.

At these scales, compute density will continue generating a tremendous amount of heat regardless of how efficient interconnects become.

Which means the real challenge of the next decade remains unchanged:
efficient heat dissipation at massive scale and doing it responsibly from an environmental standpoint.

Energy was the first bottleneck.
Cooling is becoming the second.

But the reality remains the same: gigawatts are still gigawatts. Physics does not negotiate. At these scales, compute density will continue generating a tremendous amount of heat regardless of how efficient interconnects become. Which means the real challenge of the next decade remains unchanged: efficient heat dissipation at massive scale and doing it responsibly from an environmental standpoint. Energy was the first bottleneck. Cooling is becoming the second."}}}, "rest_id": "2058630392455024874", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1424", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

AI capex spend is expected to go to "$3 to $4 trillion annually" by 2030 from $NVDA Jensen Huang projections. 

You're not bullish enough. 

And it might be a good idea to stay exposed + own the keys of the AI Kingdom:

-> $AXTI controls the materials buildout with photonics.
-> $SOI controls the AI buildout with silicon photonics.
-> $SIVE controls laser chokepoints for CPO.
-> $IQE controls Western epiwafer supply chains for photonics. 

All these started off as tiny companies, yet the trillions of projected capex gradually upward to them. 

There's many more in other industries as well. 

-> AI Capex flows to Neoclouds like $NBIS. 
-> AI Capex flows to memory like $MU and $SNDK. 

And many of the "commodity" materials or "science projects" for the past 20 years now a sudden shift in exponential TAM expansion.  

We're witnessing the next industrial revolution with Artificial Intelligence + Physical AI.

text:
The accounts pumping (and inevitably dumping) $SIVE at ~$2.3b market cap have not done their one job as providers of investment research: showing the numbers.

$SIVE had ~$33 million of revenue in 2025. 70% of it was their legacy wireless segment. The 30% of it that is photonics isn't purely data center, as that segment also includes LIDAR. A sub-division between the two categories is not given.

So, yes, they have at most $10m in DC photonics revenue and trade at least 230x that revenue today.

Now, of course, trailing revenue is a terrible way to value a photonics business, but we need an anchor somewhere with real numbers. $LITE give us plenty every single earnings call.
1. >$1b OCS run-rate in 2027.
2. Greensboro fab that supports >$5b of CPO revenue likely to be sold out.
3. $8b revenue run rate to be achieved in 18 months.

$SIVE has not announced a single DC photonics *volume* purchase order or given any DC photonics revenue guidance. Not a single one, and I'm not kidding.

The only anchor we have to go off of is that they had, at most, $10 million in data center photonics revenue in 2025, most likely far less. What CAGR is needed to justify their current $2.3 billion market cap?

text:
Photonics is not new. Fiber optic cable has crossed oceans since the 1980s.

The alpha was a physics calculation made years before this chart went vertical.

Copper degrades beyond 2 meters at 200G+ SerDes speeds. At 100K+ GPU cluster scale, NVIDIA modeled optical transceiver power consumption at 10 to 20x traditional cloud data centers. The industry's own operating philosophy was "copper if you can, optics if you must."

That "must" moment was always coming. Clusters were going to get large enough to make it physics, not preference. Once they did, the entire supply chain from InP substrates to silicon photonic foundries had years of underinvestment priced in.

The chart shows photonics vocabulary in earnings calls across NVIDIA, Coherent, Lumentum, Broadcom and six others going back to 2012. Flat for a decade. Then vertical.

The alpha was reading the physics before the rest of the market did. $AAOI $COHR $LITE $AXTI $SLOIF

text: I’ve been early on the photonics trade. I’m not questioning the trend or the technology, I question why a small Swedish company with literally ZERO proven track record in both order deliveries and manufacturing expansion and annual revenues in $30-40M and neg earnings should be valued at $3B. Competition in this field is fierce and will get even rougher. Size matters and a large player in this field should motivate a premium share price - NOT the other way around. That’s all.

text:
The AI infrastructure trade is beginning to rotate away from crowded GPU beta and into the hidden process control layers enabling advanced packaging and silicon photonics. 

$6830.TW is increasingly being reframed from a niche semiconductor analysis service company into a potential inspection and metrology proxy tied to CoWoS, InFO and optical interconnect scaling. 

As @rtk17025 points out, MSSCORPS may be one of Taiwan’s few domestic suppliers with exposure to high precision AOI and laser repair systems, giving it potential leverage to wafer defect inspection, photomasks, advanced packaging and silicon photonics. 

The key debate now is whether the company can successfully transition from a technical services model into a higher value “service + equipment + licensing” platform tied to Taiwan, Japan and U.S. silicon photonics IP.

text:
Solid take, Hunter...  $ALMU is flying under the radar. 15+ years in semis and interconnects here: GPUs steal the show, but moving massive data at scale is the real bottleneck — power, heat, and copper limits are killing efficiency. 

Aeluma’s III-V-on-silicon photonics on standard 200/300mm wafers could slash costs and scale like crazy vs. old InP tech. Strong partners (Tower, AIM, gov contracts) and real tech edge.

Early revenue, high execution risk, but at ~$414M cap, the upside in the AI optics wave is huge. 

Watch this one.Great thread. Adding to the list.(DYOR — small caps = high risk!)

text:
A team at Westlake University in China just published a handheld cancer detector in Nature Photonics. Single drop of blood, 94.9% accuracy for early-stage lung cancer, 10,000× more sensitive than the standard lab test.

Chip cost: $5.Worth paying attention to, but also worth reading carefully. That 94.9% comes from 171 patient samples. The device replaces refrigerator-sized lab equipment with an LED, a photodetector, and a 3D metamaterial chip. Genuinely impressive physics.

However, the gap nobody's talking about: every year produces 3-4 "revolutionary cancer detection" papers. Almost none survive the jump from Nature paper to clinical validation at scale.

Caveat here: 171 patients is a starting point, not proof. But the engineering approach is what's worth watching.

text: Massive gains often trigger regulatory scrutiny—expected given the timing of the Nasdaq dual-listing news. The tech is compelling, but for those of us watching the broader photonics supply chain, the real question is how they scale this versus incumbents like $LITE or $COHR. A "lottery ticket" position is one thing, but chasing this at 30x is a different game entirely.

text:
I just hit my first 10-bagger (+943%) on $SIVE in under 3 months! Discovered via @aleabitoreddit , a fundamental deep dive showed massive undervaluation in the photonics sector.

I initially bought 900 shares at 8.2 SEK, but the stock took off so fast I only managed to add a bit more below 20 SEK. The biggest lesson this investment taught me: trust your analysis and build a heavier position sooner when the conviction is there.

I locked in profits by trimming 1/3 at 49 SEK. With the price now around 85.55 SEK, I won't be adding at current valuations, but I am happily letting the rest ride as long as the company keeps delivering. Great investment, invaluable lessons.

text:
The market has figured out photonics. It’s catching on to power semis. But It still hasn’t woken up to fiber. 

The real chokepoint is sitting in the dirt. I'm positioned accordingly in $HLIT.

“Global fiber prices have risen ~70% from a 2021 trough of $3.70 to ~$6.30 per fiber-kilometer.”
 — Tom’s Hardware

“North American demand growth is projected at 22% to 25% this year, while supply expansion trails at 12% to 19%.”  
— Rebio Group estimates, reported by Tom’s Hardware

“That dual demand [AI + BEAD] elevates our fiber forecast, but it also makes the shortage more pronounced.”  
— Anis Khemakhem, Clearfield

“AI-focused data centers require significantly more fiber — about 36x more fiber than traditional CPU-based racks — to handle the massive data volumes and high-speed connectivity required by GPU clusters.”  
— Rahul Puri, CEO Optical Networking, STL

“Data center fiber demand grew roughly 76% year-on-year in 2025… the segment is projected to account for 30% of total global fiber demand by 2027. In 2024, that figure was below 5%.”
 — CRU data, reported by Tom’s Hardware

“AI has pushed fiber demand to levels we’ve never seen to ensure the incredible speeds inside data centers and between campuses. The many, massive data center builds we’re seeing today are driving huge volumes of intra-bay, inter-bay and middle-mile fiber.”
 — Anis Khemakhem, CCO, Clearfield

“The surge in hyperscale and AI network loads has significantly increased our expectations for fiber demand. Enterprise sales grew 58% year-over-year in Q3 2025, driven by continued strong adoption of Corning’s Gen AI products.”
 — John McGirr, Corning

“The U.S. will need to add 213.3 million more fiber miles by 2029, more than doubling its current amount from 159.6 million fiber miles to 372.9 million miles.”
 — Rahul Puri, STL

Major beneficiaries include:
$HLIT, $NOK, $LUMN, $CXDO, $CLFD, $BDC, $GLW
embedded_text:
$HLIT  reminds me strongly of early $ASTS.

If AST SpaceMobile is the company building the connectivity layer from space, $HLIT (Harmonic) is its terrestrial mirror. The platform layer powers every fiber and cable network on the ground. Companies that build real technical moats https://t.co/XE86WwkQ1u

In an environment where every ISP is racing to upgrade to DOCSIS 4.0 and deploy fiber, a platform that manages both from one stack is becoming table stakes, and $HLIT already has it. text:
A closer and objective look at the hard risks facing $POET Technologies:

While the hype around AI and silicon photonics has driven a massive rally, investors need to look closely at the underlying numbers and events before chasing the run.

First is the massive gap between the valuation and actual business results. The market cap has hovered around 2.4B USD, yet the company generated just over 500K USD in revenue last quarter. Meanwhile, they lost 12.3M USD, carrying an ongoing quarterly operational cash burn of nearly 9M USD.

Second is the severe history of share dilution. Shares outstanding exploded by over 300% in recent years. This was capped off by a massive 400M USD direct offering on May 15, which immediately dilutes existing holders and could expand the share base by up to 25% if the accompanying warrants are fully exercised.

Third is the operational instability and key customer losses. The stock crashed nearly 50% in late April after Marvell Semiconductor canceled its Celestial AI-related purchase orders. The reason given was a direct breach of confidentiality by POET's own management, who discussed order details during a public YouTube interview.

Fourth is the scathing short report alleging that the headline-grabbing 50M USD Lumilens order is with an empty corporate shell that has zero independent patents. Field reports from industry conferences also suggest key purported partners like Foxconn and Luxshare actually have inactive or non-existent relationships.

Finally, POET is facing multiple class-action lawsuits over these NDA disclosures and historical tax classification issues. Despite the optimistic commentary, even Wall Street analysts have an average target price of around 8.36, implying huge downside from current trading levels.

If you are playing this strictly as a short-term momentum trade, chasing the run is a perfectly fine strategy as long as you keep a tight stop loss and do not get married to the position. But for anyone looking at this as a long-term investment, the operational track record, structural dilution, and relationship failures make it an incredibly high-risk bet at these valuations.

First is the massive gap between the valuation and actual business results. The market cap has hovered around 2.4B USD, yet the company generated just over 500K USD in revenue last quarter. Meanwhile, they lost 12.3M USD, carrying an ongoing quarterly operational cash burn of nearly 9M USD. Second is the severe history of share dilution. Shares outstanding exploded by over 300% in recent years. This was capped off by a massive 400M USD direct offering on May 15, which immediately dilutes existing holders and could expand the share base by up to 25% if the accompanying warrants are fully exercised. Third is the operational instability and key customer losses. The stock crashed nearly 50% in late April after Marvell Semiconductor canceled its Celestial AI-related purchase orders. The reason given was a direct breach of confidentiality by POET's own management, who discussed order details during a public YouTube interview. Fourth is the scathing short report alleging that the headline-grabbing 50M USD Lumilens order is with an empty corporate shell that has zero independent patents. Field reports from industry conferences also suggest key purported partners like Foxconn and Luxshare actually have inactive or non-existent relationships. Finally, POET is facing multiple class-action lawsuits over these NDA disclosures and historical tax classification issues. Despite the optimistic commentary, even Wall Street analysts have an average target price of around 8.36, implying huge downside from current trading levels. If you are playing this strictly as a short-term momentum trade, chasing the run is a perfectly fine strategy as long as you keep a tight stop loss and do not get married to the position. But for anyone looking at this as a long-term investment, the operational track record, structural dilution, and relationship failures make it an incredibly high-risk bet at these valuations."}}}, "rest_id": "2058973000615989566", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1271", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Before turning to photonics, I tried my luck in biotech. The field caught my interest quite strongly, and for about two years I read almost exclusively books in that area. Overall, I had a fairly good track record.

I don’t own any of the four stocks $QURE, $CMPS, $CAPR, or https://t.co/zgqEYAzfFg, anymore. Back then, all four were asymmetric bets for me, and contrary to the odds, three of them worked out very well (multibaggers), while even the fourth still ended up slightly positive.

A few weeks ago, I made another biotech investment where I see an asymmetric opportunity. Unfortunately, I can’t share it here, even though I’ve written a full write-up on it. The company is simply too small, and sharing it would likely drive the price up significantly. Ideally, it should re-rate for fundamental reasons, not because of attention or momentum. In cases like this, a paid Substack model would actually make sense.

Once the case plays out, either successfully or not, I’ll share it here. That way it will be documented whether my “biotech touch” is still intact.

Many people probably associate me primarily with photonics, but in reality I’m more of a generalist covering a range of different fields. I try to avoid having only a single hammer in the toolbox, otherwise every problem starts looking like a nail.

but I wanted to write something up because of the pictured quote inspired me * When I first invested in $LPTH over a year ago, my initial thesis was largely driven by geopolitical tailwinds, especially China’s germanium export restrictions and the implications for domestic supply chains. But the reason I’ve continued to hold and add more shares very quickly evolved. And one aspect that truly strengthened my conviction in my investment is the leadership of @skrubin. Under his direction, the company has very clearly positioned itself at the forefront of EO/IR systems, threat detection, and advanced optics... markets with durable, long-term demand driven by national security and technological acceleration. And this current company positioning is not solely a case of “preparation meeting opportunity” with BlackDiamond serving as an alternative to germanium at the right moment. While that was an important inflection point no doubt, what gives me greater confidence going forward now is how Sam is actively compounding that advantage. He’s iterating on the core technology with next-generation BDNL materials, expanding applications into air defense and space-based systems, strengthening relationships with prime contractors like $LMT, $LHX, $ESLT and Anduril, pursuing strategic M&A (G5 and AMI), and methodically moving the company further up the value chain. These distinctions truly matter as some leaders benefit from favorable macro conditions while others build enduring platforms on top of them. Sam is clearly doing the latter. He’s turning a geopolitical tailwind into a structural competitive advantage and that’s what sets up LightPath not just to participate in this current cycle, but to compound through it. I truly think that the quote “The secret to investing is to invest in unstoppable people building inevitable things” genuinely applies to @skrubin and it clearly comes across with every investor engagement he participates in and not because of any sort of hype, but because of his clarity, his strategic intent, and consistent execution. He’s one of the few CEOs whose every single interview, earnings call, and even social media update I genuinely look forward to as an investor. Leaders like Sam are how companies transform and flourish, and I truly believe that over the next 5–10 years, LightPath has the potential to become the next FLIR Systems (now part Teledyne) or Leonardo DRS."}}}, "rest_id": "2025957796156510217", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "29114", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057884513276240309", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2977", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

It has now been two months since I published my first deep dive on $SIVE 

The stock is up 10x since then and, honestly, I think it is now massively overextended.

SEK 21.7B market cap on SEK 306M of revenue.
More than 70x sales.

I remain bullish on the long-term photonics / AI connectivity thesis, and I was probably one of the first public bulls talking about the company. But even then, the current valuation is absolutely astronomical.

At this point, the market is already pricing in a brutal revenue ramp, massive conversion from NRE into product revenue, and near-perfect execution over the coming years.

That said, I’m not fucking selling

$COHR, $MTSI, $STM and Hamamatsu quietly dominating SpaceX’s lasercomm supply chain is another real-world proof point that photonics demand is expanding beyond terrestrial data centers.

the post maps the space-qualified chain perfectly; precision optics/coatings/EDFAs, high-speed drivers/TIAs, PAT control ASICs, and high-sensitivity detectors. 

flight heritage is the ultimate moat in this market, and these four are already winning the first generation.

this is bullish for the broader photonics layer. 

hyperscalers need reliable low-latency global connectivity for AI data centers, edge computing, and the full physical stack. starlink-type orbital networks solve real bottlenecks that terrestrial grids can’t fix fast enough. the same laser and optical engine technology that powers inter-satellite links has direct read-through to terrestrial CPO and pluggable ramps.

this cycle rewards the execution plays solving proven physical bottlenecks that hyperscalers literally can’t pause for. photonics (lasers, optics, detectors) is one of those constraints, and the space demand layer just adds another structural tailwind on top of the terrestrial AI buildout.

the setup has legs. this one ages well.

text:
IPG Photonics shares moved lower Wednesday after the company disclosed insider selling activity by CFO Timothy P.V. Mammen. According to a Form 4 filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, Mammen sold a total of 9,975 shares in two separate transactions at weighted average prices of $119.60 and $120.37 per share. 
The first sale included 7,263 shares sold at prices ranging from $119.05 to $119.92, while the second transaction involved 2,712 shares sold between $120.25 and $120.49 per share. After the transactions, Mammen continues to directly own 76,438 shares of IPG Photonics stock. 
Although insider sales can occur for many personal or financial planning reasons, investors often closely watch executive transactions for potential signals regarding management sentiment and the company’s near-term outlook. The disclosure added pressure to shares during Wednesday’s session.  
$IPGP

text:
$MRVL  just dropped Q1. Recorded $2.418B revenue, $0.80 EPS, raised the full year and even guided FY28 higher. Stock up 2% AH.
Honestly,

The print is solid, but the real story is photonics. Their 1.6T optics, 51.2T Ethernet switches, and co-packaged optical stuff for scale-up AI clusters are the actual differentiators. Everyone wants the XPU headlines, but the interconnect is where Marvell becomes irreplaceable.

Listening for any Trainium 2 colour on the call. Also waiting to hear if Microsoft Maia or any Meta MTIA work gets a name dropped. Murphy has been coy about it, but the booking number he keeps hinting at has to come from somewhere.

This is a call you actually want to listen to live.

The @CommerceGov Department recently awarded PsiQuantum $100M to accelerate development of BTO, giving a rare look into the underlying manufacturing stack required to scale fault-tolerant quantum computing. We profiled PsiQuantum last September following its $1B Series E, including how the company produces the world’s highest-performing optical switch - a core component of its silicon photonics platform. PsiQuantum is currently the only company or institution in the world manufacturing this BTO material for optical switches at 300mm scale. While the company built this silicon photonics platform for fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC)*, the implications may extend far beyond quantum itself. As AI data centers increasingly shift from copper to optical networking, PsiQuantum’s photonics stack could also become foundational infrastructure for next-generation AI systems. *Fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) refers to quantum computers that can continue operating accurately even when individual quantum bits (“qubits”) are noisy or error-prone.
